text.title: Feature Discovery Examples and Antipatterns
text.summary: Two worked examples (SaaS dashboard, mobile app DAU) and six common mistakes with concrete fixes.

example.note: Problem: dashboard too cluttered, key metrics buried. Discovery: 15 interviews ("what do you look for first?"), analytics (80% of users view only 3 of 12 widgets), 23 support tickets requesting simpler view. Features identified with RICE: Customizable dashboard (1200), Focus mode (900), Saved views (600). Validation: prototype test with 10 users, 8/10 preferred simplified view. Decision: build Focus mode first — highest RICE, lowest effort.

Problem: low daily active users after download. Discovery: churn interviews ("I forget the app exists"), analytics (90% never enable notifications), competitor analysis (push notifications with value). Features by Opportunity Score: Smart notifications (16), Daily digest (12), Widget for home screen (10). Decision: A/B test notification types first, then build widget.

antipattern.description: Building every feature request without prioritization.
antipattern.reason: Feature bloat reduces the product's core value proposition; every added feature increases maintenance cost and user confusion.

antipattern.description: No validation step between RICE scoring and engineering commitment.
antipattern.reason: RICE scores are estimates; without behavioral validation, the team builds on assumptions, not evidence.

antipattern.description: Copying a competitor feature without understanding why they built it.
antipattern.reason: A competitor feature may exist for a different customer segment or job; copying it without context wastes effort and may confuse your users.

antipattern.description: Weighting feature requests equally regardless of customer segment value.
antipattern.reason: A feature requested by 50 free-tier users may be less valuable than one requested by 5 enterprise customers; segment value must factor into prioritization.

antipattern.description: Generating RICE confidence scores without explicit evidence assessment.
antipattern.reason: Agent-generated confidence scores are opinions, not evidence assessments; confidence must reflect the quality and quantity of actual validation data collected.

rule.statement: Separate "what customers ask for" from "what job they need done" before writing any feature spec. Request: "export to Excel" — Job: "share data with my finance team."
rule.rationale: Building the stated request often misses better solutions to the underlying job; understanding the job opens a wider solution space.

text.reference: LLM prompt to extract feature candidates from support tickets
LLM prompt to compute Opportunity Scores
